section.title: It Is Good to Praise the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The only psalm titled explicitly for the Sabbath, this song contrasts the brief flourishing of the wicked, who sprout like grass only to be destroyed, with the righteous, who are planted in the house of the LORD and flourish even in old age, a truth resting finally on Christ, the Rock in whom there is no unrighteousness.
section.thesis: It is good to praise the LORD morning and night for His great works, trusting that though the wicked flourish briefly like grass, the righteous, planted in God's house, will bear fruit even in old age, a flourishing rooted in Christ the Rock, in whom there is no unrighteousness.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the call to praise God morning and night, verses 1-3; joy in the greatness of God's works, verses 4-5; the certain, sudden end of the wicked, verses 6-9; the vindication and anointing of the righteous, verses 10-11; and the righteous flourishing like a palm tree in God's house, verses 12-15.

units.label: Morning and Night Praise
units.note: the call to praise on the Sabbath

verses.text: It is good to praise the LORD, and to sing praises to Your name, O {{elyon|Most High}},
to proclaim Your loving devotion in the morning and Your faithfulness at night
with the ten-stringed harp and the melody of the lyre.

units.label: How Great Are Your Works
units.note: joy in the greatness of God's works

verses.text: For You, O LORD, have made me glad by Your deeds; I sing for joy at the works of Your hands.
How great are Your works, O LORD, how deep are Your thoughts!

units.label: They Will Be Forever Destroyed
units.note: the certain, sudden end of the wicked

verses.text: A senseless man does not know, and a fool does not understand,
that though the wicked sprout like grass, and all evildoers flourish, they will be forever destroyed.
But You, O LORD, are exalted forever!
For surely Your enemies, O LORD, surely Your enemies will perish; all evildoers will be scattered.
But You have exalted my horn like that of a wild ox; with fine oil I have been anointed.
My eyes see the downfall of my enemies; my ears hear the wailing of my wicked foes.

units.label: Flourishing Like a Palm Tree
units.note: the righteous planted in God's house

verses.text: The {{tsaddik|righteous}} will flourish like a {{tamar|palm tree}}, and grow like a cedar in Lebanon.
Planted in the house of the LORD, they will flourish in the courts of our God.
In old age they will still bear fruit; healthy and green they will remain,
to proclaim, "The LORD is upright; He is my {{tsur|Rock}}, and in Him there is no unrighteousness."

tsaddik.gloss: Righteous, one who is just or in right standing. The psalm pictures such a person not as flawless in themselves but as one rooted, planted, and dependent on the LORD for their flourishing.

tamar.gloss: A palm tree, known in Israel for its long life and continued fruitfulness even into great age, a fitting picture of the enduring vitality God grants the righteous.

elyon.gloss: Most High, a divine title emphasizing God's supremacy over every rival power. The psalm's opening call to praise is addressed directly to God under this exalted name.

tsur.gloss: Rock, a picture of stability, refuge, and unshakeable strength. The psalm closes by naming the LORD this Rock, in whom there is no unrighteousness, a title the New Testament applies to Christ.

basic.context: Titled a song for the Sabbath day, this psalm calls worshipers to praise God morning and night with music, then contrasts the wicked, who sprout quickly like grass only to be destroyed, with the righteous, who are planted in God's house and flourish with fruit even into old age.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a real, lived contrast between the fleeting prosperity of the wicked and the rooted flourishing of the righteous, and it prescribes an abiding pattern of worship, marking every day, and especially the Sabbath, with deliberate praise for God's works.
basic.originalLanguages: This shabbat, Sabbath, song calls the tsaddik, righteous, verse 12, to flourish like a tamar, palm tree, known for bearing fruit even in old age, and closes by naming the LORD as tsur, Rock, verse 15, the foundation of stability in whom there is no unrighteousness.
basic.typology: The righteous 'planted in the house of the LORD,' verse 13, anticipates believers as living stones built into a spiritual house, and the psalm's closing title, 'the Rock,' points forward to Christ, the spiritual Rock from which His people drink.
basic.christ: Paul identifies the Rock that accompanied Israel as Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4, and this psalm's closing confession, 'He is my Rock, and in Him there is no unrighteousness,' finds its fullest truth in Jesus, the only truly righteous Rock on whom His people's flourishing depends.
basic.god: God is shown as the one whose works are great and whose thoughts are deep beyond the fool's understanding, exalted forever above every enemy, and faithful to make the righteous flourish and endure precisely because they are rooted in Him rather than in themselves.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:4 names Christ as the spiritual Rock. 1 Peter 2:5 describes believers as living stones built into a spiritual house. Hebrews 4:9-10 speaks of a Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This Sabbath song's contrast between the fleeting wicked and the enduringly fruitful righteous, planted in God's house and resting on Him as their Rock, points forward to Christ, the true Rock in whom believers are planted and in whom alone there is no unrighteousness.

context.literary: A song explicitly titled for the Sabbath, moving from a call to worship, verses 1-3, to personal joy in God's works, verses 4-5, a contrast between the wicked and the LORD's eternal exaltation, verses 6-11, and the flourishing of the righteous, verses 12-15.
context.historical: Used in Second Temple worship as the psalm for the Sabbath day, read in the temple courts and paired liturgically with the two daily sacrifices, giving weekly rhythm to Israel's praise of God's works.
context.canonical: Standing early in Book IV, which repeatedly emphasizes God's eternal kingship after the crisis of Psalm 89, this psalm grounds confidence not in any earthly throne but in worship itself and in being planted, like a tree, in the LORD's own house.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people to deliberate, rhythmic praise, especially on the Sabbath, and to assure them that despite the brief, grass-like flourishing of the wicked, those planted in the LORD's house will endure and bear fruit even in old age.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a general pattern observable in life, wicked prosperity that proves temporary, righteous endurance rooted in God; it prescribes ongoing worship and trust in God's justice rather than envy of present, passing success.

debates.question: Does verse 7's promise that the wicked 'will be forever destroyed' describe a specific historical judgment or a final, eschatological one?

views.label: It primarily describes the recurring pattern of temporal judgment on the wicked within history
views.case: The surrounding wisdom language, comparing the wicked to quickly sprouting grass, echoes Psalm 37 and 73's observations about the fleeting nature of wicked prosperity within ordinary life and history.

views.label: It points forward to final, eschatological judgment and destruction
views.case: The absolute language, 'forever destroyed,' paired with the LORD being 'exalted forever,' verse 8, suggests a judgment as permanent and complete as God's own eternal reign, pointing beyond any single historical downfall.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that present appearances are deceptive, wicked flourishing is always temporary, and the righteous can trust God's justice whether it unfolds within history or is completed only at the final judgment.

study.originalLanguages: This song for the shabbat, Sabbath, calls the tsaddik, righteous, verse 12, to flourish like a tamar, palm tree, prized for bearing fruit even in advanced age, and closes with the confession that the LORD is tsur, Rock, verse 15, in whom there is no unrighteousness, a title later applied directly to Christ.

typology.type: Planted in the house of the LORD, flourishing in His courts, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house, 1 Peter 2:5, rooted permanently in Christ.

typology.type: The LORD as Rock, in whom there is no unrighteousness, verse 15
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, identified by Paul as the spiritual Rock, 1 Corinthians 10:4, the only truly righteous foundation for His people.

study.christ: This psalm's closing confession, that the LORD is our Rock and in Him there is no unrighteousness, verse 15, finds its fullest truth in Jesus Christ, whom Paul names as the spiritual Rock that accompanied Israel, 1 Corinthians 10:4. Just as the righteous are planted in God's house and flourish even into old age, so believers, built into Christ as living stones, are rooted in Him and bear fruit that does not fail.
study.god: God's works are great and His thoughts deep beyond the fool's grasp, verse 5. He is exalted forever above every enemy, ensures the wicked's flourishing is only temporary, and causes the righteous, who are rooted and planted in Him rather than in themselves, to bear fruit even into old age.

crossReferences.note: Paul identifies Christ as the spiritual Rock, fulfilling this psalm's closing title for the LORD.
Believers as living stones built into a spiritual house echoes the righteous being planted in the house of the LORD.
A Sabbath rest remains for the people of God, fulfilled in the greater rest found in Christ.

study.oneStory: This Sabbath song's confidence that the righteous, planted in God's house, will flourish long after the wicked's brief prosperity has withered, rests finally on Christ, the Rock in whom there is no unrighteousness, in whom His people are rooted and bear lasting fruit.

quiz.q: What is this psalm uniquely titled for, according to its heading?

options.text: A song for the Sabbath day.
options.note: Psalm 92 is the only psalm in the Psalter explicitly titled for the Sabbath.

options.text: A song for a coronation.
options.note: The heading names the Sabbath, not a coronation, as the occasion.

options.text: A song for a funeral.
options.note: The psalm's tone is joyful praise, not funeral lament.

options.text: A song of general praise and thanksgiving to God.
options.note: This is true of its content and tone, but the heading specifically names the Sabbath day as its occasion, unique among all the psalms.

quiz.q: What comparison does verse 7 use to describe the wicked?

options.text: They sprout like grass and flourish briefly, but will be forever destroyed.
options.note: The verse contrasts the wicked's quick, temporary flourishing with their certain, lasting destruction.

options.text: They are like cedar trees that last for centuries.
options.note: Cedar imagery in this psalm describes the righteous, not the wicked.

options.text: They never experience any success at all.
options.note: The verse acknowledges the wicked do flourish, though only briefly.

options.text: They prosper for a season but face inevitable decline.
options.note: This is essentially correct in substance, though the verse's specific and vivid image is grass that sprouts quickly and is just as quickly destroyed forever.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the righteous in verses 12-14?

options.text: Flourishing like a palm tree, planted in God's house, bearing fruit even in old age.
options.note: These verses picture enduring vitality and fruitfulness rooted in being planted in the LORD's house.

options.text: Withering quickly like grass.
options.note: That description belongs to the wicked, not the righteous, in this psalm.

options.text: Flourishing only in youth, then fading entirely.
options.note: The psalm specifically says the righteous still bear fruit in old age.

options.text: Growing strong and enduring like a great tree.
options.note: This is true and close to correct, but the psalm's fuller picture specifically names being planted in the house of the LORD as the source of that enduring strength.

quiz.q: What title does the psalm give the LORD in its closing verse?

options.text: My Rock, in whom there is no unrighteousness.
options.note: Verse 15 closes with this confession of God's uprightness and stability.

options.text: My enemy, to be feared and avoided.
options.note: The psalm's closing confession is one of trust and praise, not fear or avoidance.

options.text: A distant judge with no relationship to His people.
options.note: The title 'my Rock' expresses close, personal trust, not distance.

options.text: A God who is entirely just and reliable.
options.note: This is true and closely related, but the psalm's exact closing words specifically name Him 'my Rock,' emphasizing close, personal trust, not only abstract justice.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament connect to this psalm's closing image of the Rock?

options.text: Paul identifies Christ Himself as the spiritual Rock that accompanied Israel.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 10:4 names Christ as this Rock, fulfilling the psalm's closing confession.

options.text: The New Testament never uses rock imagery for God or Christ.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 10:4 explicitly uses this very image, applying it to Christ.

options.text: It connects only to a literal geological formation in Israel.
options.note: The connection is theological and christological, not merely geological.

options.text: It anticipates a sure foundation on which God's people can safely stand.
options.note: This is true in a general sense, but the precise New Testament connection is Paul's direct identification of Christ Himself as this spiritual Rock, 1 Corinthians 10:4.

questions: This psalm calls for praise both morning and night. What would it look like for you to deliberately mark the beginning and end of your day with praise to God?
The wicked are described as sprouting like grass, flourishing briefly before being destroyed. Have you ever envied the apparent, short-lived success of those who ignore God?
The righteous are pictured as planted in God's house, not growing on their own. Where do you tend to seek your own strength instead of staying rooted in God's presence and worship?
Verse 14 promises the righteous will still bear fruit in old age. How does this promise encourage you, whether you are early or late in life, about ongoing fruitfulness for God?
The psalm closes by calling God 'my Rock, in whom there is no unrighteousness.' How does trusting Christ as this Rock change the way you face uncertainty or temptation this week?

translationNotes.note: This is the only psalm in the Psalter explicitly titled 'A song for the Sabbath day,' and was read in Second Temple worship on the seventh day.
The horn exalted like a wild ox is an idiom for renewed strength and honor, and fine oil pictures anointing for blessing and joy.
The palm tree was valued in the ancient Near East for continuing to bear dates for many decades, making it a fitting image for enduring fruitfulness in old age.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 92:1-15, 15 verses). No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed this session as a dedicated message on Psalm 92, so status is none.
